section.title: The Foolish Shepherd
section.sectionRationale: The dark heart of the first burden. Zechariah acts out the role of a good shepherd of a doomed flock, is rejected, and is paid off with thirty pieces of silver, thrown to the potter in the house of the LORD. Matthew sees this fulfilled in Judas's betrayal price for Christ. When the good Shepherd is spurned, a worthless shepherd is raised up instead.
section.thesis: When God's people reject the good Shepherd and value Him at the contemptible price of a slave, they forfeit favor and union and are handed over to a worthless shepherd, a rejection fulfilled when Christ was betrayed for thirty pieces of silver.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the wailing over the doomed land; the shepherd of the flock marked for slaughter, his two staffs Favor and Union broken, and the thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter; and the raising up of the worthless shepherd, ending in woe upon him.

verses.text: Open your doors, O Lebanon, that the fire may consume your cedars!
Wail, O cypress, for the cedar has fallen; the majestic trees are ruined! Wail, O oaks of Bashan, for the dense forest has been cut down!
Listen to the wailing of the shepherds, for their glory is in ruins. Listen to the roaring of the young lions, for the thickets of the Jordan are destroyed.
This is what the LORD my God says: “Pasture the flock marked for slaughter,
whose buyers slaughter them without remorse. Those who sell them say, ‘Blessed be the LORD, for I am rich!’ Even their own shepherds have no compassion on them.

For I will no longer have compassion on the people of the land, declares the LORD, but behold, I will cause each man to fall into the hands of his neighbor and his king, who will devastate the land, and I will not deliver it from their hands.”

So I pastured the flock marked for slaughter, especially the afflicted of the flock. Then I took for myself two staffs, calling one {{noam|Favor}} and the other {{chovlim|Union}}, and I pastured the flock.
And in one month I dismissed three shepherds. My soul grew impatient with the flock, and their souls also detested me.
Then I said, “I will no longer shepherd you. Let the dying die, and the perishing perish; and let those who remain devour one another's flesh.”
Next I took my staff called Favor and cut it in two, revoking the covenant I had made with all the nations.
It was revoked on that day, and so the afflicted of the flock who were watching me knew that it was the word of the LORD.
Then I told them, “If it seems right to you, give me my wages; but if not, keep them.” So they weighed out my wages, thirty pieces of {{keseph|silver}}.

And the LORD said to me, “Throw it to the potter,” this magnificent price at which they valued me. So I took the thirty pieces of silver and threw them to the {{yotser|potter}} in the house of the LORD.
Then I cut in two my second staff called Union, breaking the brotherhood between Judah and Israel.

units.label: The worthless shepherd
units.note: woe to the one who deserts the flock

verses.text: And the LORD said to me: “Take up once more the equipment of a foolish shepherd.

For behold, I will raise up a shepherd in the land who will neither care for the lost, nor seek the young, nor heal the broken, nor sustain the healthy, but he will devour the flesh of the choice sheep and tear off their hooves.
Woe to the worthless shepherd, who deserts the flock! May a sword strike his arm and his right eye! May his arm be completely withered and his right eye utterly blinded!”

noam.gloss: Favor, pleasantness, graciousness, delight. The first shepherd's staff is named Favor, representing God's gracious protection of His flock, including a restraining covenant over the nations. Cutting it in two dramatizes the withdrawal of that gracious protection when the good Shepherd is rejected, leaving the flock exposed to the nations.

chovlim.gloss: Bonds, ties, union. The second staff is named Union, representing the God-given brotherhood binding His people together, especially Judah and Israel. Breaking it pictures the shattering of that unity, the community torn apart, when they spurn their Shepherd. Grace withdrawn and union broken are the twin consequences of rejecting the good Shepherd.

keseph.gloss: Silver, money. Thirty pieces of silver was the compensation for a slave gored by an ox in the law of Exodus, a contemptibly low valuation. To weigh out this sum as the Shepherd's wages is to insult Him, valuing the LORD's servant at the price of a slave. Matthew sees the exact price fulfilled in the thirty silver coins paid to betray Jesus.

yotser.gloss: A potter, a maker of clay vessels. Throwing the silver to the potter in the house of the LORD signals contemptuous disposal of a shameful price, discarded to a lowly craftsman at the temple. Matthew records that the betrayal money was used to buy the potter's field, tying this action directly to the burial ground purchased with Judas's coins.

basic.context: This is the darkest chapter of the first burden. After a poem wailing over a doomed land, Zechariah is told to act out the role of a shepherd over a flock marked for slaughter, a flock exploited by its sellers and its own shepherds. He takes two staffs, Favor and Union. The flock detests him, so he resigns, breaks the staff Favor, and asks for his wages. They weigh out thirty pieces of silver, the price of a slave, an insult. God tells him to throw it to the potter in the temple. He breaks Union too. Then God raises up a worthless shepherd instead, and pronounces woe on him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a prophetic sign-act, acted out and heavy with symbolism. The good shepherd represents God's true care rejected by the people. The two staffs picture God's gracious protection and the people's unity, both broken by their rejection. The thirty pieces of silver and the potter are read by Matthew as fulfilled in the betrayal of Jesus. The worthless shepherd raised up in place of the good one warns of judgment through bad leadership.
basic.originalLanguages: The two staffs are named noam, Favor, God's gracious protection, and chovlim, Union, the bond of brotherhood, both cut in two. The Shepherd's wage is thirty pieces of keseph, silver, the compensation for a slave, a contemptuous price. And it is thrown to the yotser, the potter, in the temple, an image of shameful disposal Matthew ties to the potter's field.
basic.typology: The good shepherd rejected and valued at a slave's price is fulfilled in Jesus, betrayed for thirty pieces of silver. The money thrown to the potter is fulfilled in the potter's field bought with the betrayal money. The worthless shepherd raised up when the good one is spurned points toward the ultimate false shepherd, the antichrist, who devours rather than feeds.
basic.christ: Matthew explicitly connects this chapter to Christ. The thirty pieces of silver weighed out as the Shepherd's insulting wage is the exact sum Judas received to betray Jesus, and the coins, thrown down in the temple, were used to buy the potter's field. The good Shepherd, priced at the value of a slave and rejected by His own, is the Lord Jesus. When He is spurned, grace and union are broken, and a devouring shepherd takes His place.
basic.god: God feels the insult of rejected shepherd-love, and He is just in the consequences: when His care is despised, His gracious protection and the people's unity are withdrawn, and they are handed over to leaders who exploit them. Yet even the price of His Shepherd's rejection becomes part of His sovereign plan, foretold in exact detail and fulfilled in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 27 records the thirty pieces of silver and the potter's field, citing this prophecy. Matthew 26 tells of Judas's betrayal price. John 1 says He came to His own and His own did not receive Him. John 10 contrasts the good Shepherd with the hireling who abandons the sheep.
basic.oneStory: The good Shepherd is rejected and valued at a slave's price, and grace and union are broken, so a worthless shepherd is raised up. This is fulfilled when Christ is betrayed for thirty pieces of silver, spurned by His own. The story bends toward the cross, where the rejected Shepherd lays down His life, and warns of the false shepherd who must finally be judged.

context.literary: The climax and turning point of the first burden, following the coming King of chapter 9 and the restoration of the flock in chapter 10, and now showing the tragic rejection of the Shepherd. The shepherd theme that ran through those chapters darkens here into betrayal, setting up the struck Shepherd of chapter 13 and the mourning of chapter 12.
context.historical: The sign-act reflects Israel's long history of rejecting God's true leadership and being handed over to exploitative rulers, foreign and domestic. The three dismissed shepherds and the internal strife picture the failure of leadership in the community. The prophecy transcends its setting, pointing forward to the decisive rejection of the Messiah.
context.canonical: The chapter is one of the most striking predictive prophecies in the Old Testament, cited in Matthew 27 for the thirty pieces of silver and the potter's field. It joins Ezekiel 34, Jeremiah 23, and John 10 in the biblical theme of good and false shepherds, and it deepens the portrait of the rejected and struck Shepherd fulfilled in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To dramatize, through a prophetic sign-act, the people's rejection of God's good shepherd, the resulting withdrawal of gracious protection and unity, the contemptible price at which the Shepherd is valued, and the judgment of being handed over to a worthless, devouring shepherd.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The sign-act is prophetic revelation, not a pattern to imitate. The abiding truths are the tragedy and guilt of rejecting God's true Shepherd, the just consequences of that rejection, and the precise fulfillment of the prophecy in Christ's betrayal.

debates.question: Who is the worthless shepherd raised up in verses 15 to 17?

views.label: Exploitative leadership as judgment
views.case: When the people reject the good Shepherd, God gives them over to selfish leaders who devour rather than feed, a fitting judgment on those who spurned true care.

views.label: A pointer to the ultimate false shepherd
views.case: The devouring shepherd who deserts the flock anticipates the final antichrist figure, the false shepherd who stands over against Christ the good Shepherd and is judged with woe.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage warns that rejecting the true Shepherd leads to bondage under false shepherds, who face God's woe.

debates.question: How does Matthew's use of this text relate to Jeremiah?

views.label: A blended citation under Jeremiah's name
views.case: Matthew 27 names Jeremiah while drawing chiefly on Zechariah 11, a recognized practice of citing a prophetic collection by its major prophet while combining passages, including Jeremiah's potter and field imagery.

views.label: Zechariah as the primary source
views.case: The thirty pieces of silver and the potter come directly from Zechariah 11, which is the controlling text behind Matthew's fulfillment statement about Judas's betrayal price.

debates.bearing: Both agree that Zechariah 11 stands behind Matthew's account of the thirty pieces of silver and the potter's field, fulfilled in Christ's betrayal.

study.originalLanguages: Four words carry the tragedy. The good Shepherd takes two staffs, noam, Favor, standing for God's gracious protection over His flock and a restraining hand on the nations, and chovlim, Union, the God-given bond of brotherhood binding His people together. When He is rejected He cuts both in two: grace withdrawn and unity shattered. Then His wage is weighed out, thirty pieces of keseph, silver, the very sum the law of Exodus set as compensation for a slave killed by an ox, a contemptibly low price that insults the Shepherd by valuing Him at a slave's worth. And He is told to throw it to the yotser, the potter, in the house of the LORD, a gesture of disgusted disposal that Matthew records was literally fulfilled when the betrayal coins bought the potter's field. Every term drives toward the rejection of Christ.

typology.type: The good shepherd valued at thirty pieces of silver
typology.fulfillment: Jesus betrayed for thirty pieces of silver, the price of a slave, Matthew 26:15 and 27:9.

typology.type: The silver thrown to the potter in the temple
typology.fulfillment: The betrayal money cast into the temple and used to buy the potter's field, Matthew 27:5-10.

typology.type: The worthless shepherd who devours the flock
typology.fulfillment: The false shepherd set against Christ the good Shepherd, judged with woe, John 10:12-13 and 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

study.christ: This chapter is one of the most remarkable predictive prophecies in all of Scripture, and Matthew tells us plainly that it points to Jesus. The good Shepherd pastures a flock marked for slaughter, gives Himself to the afflicted of the flock, and is met with contempt; when He asks for His wages, they weigh out thirty pieces of silver, the price of a slave, a deliberate insult to the LORD's servant. The Lord commands that the shameful sum be thrown to the potter in the house of the LORD. Centuries later Judas agreed to betray Jesus for thirty pieces of silver, flung the coins down in the temple, and the chief priests used them to buy the potter's field as a burial place, all of which Matthew says fulfilled the word spoken through the prophet. Here, then, is the good Shepherd of John 10, come to His own and rejected by His own, priced at the value of a slave, betrayed and handed over. And when the true Shepherd is spurned, the passage warns, a worthless, devouring shepherd is left in His place, until he too meets the woe of God. The whole chapter is the shadow of Gethsemane and the thirty silver coins.
study.god: The God of this sign-act enters into the pain of rejected love. He acts the part of the faithful Shepherd who pastures even the afflicted of a doomed flock, and He feels the sting when His people value Him at a slave's price and detest His care. His justice is real: when His gracious protection and the gift of unity are despised, He withdraws them, cutting Favor and Union in two, and hands the people over to leaders who will devour them, a sober judgment on those who spurn the true Shepherd. Yet even in this darkness His sovereignty shines, for He foretells the very price of His Shepherd's betrayal, the potter, and the field, centuries before Judas, so that the rejection of Christ, freely and guiltily chosen, still falls within the saving plan of God. And the closing woe on the worthless shepherd assures us that God will not leave His abused flock without vindication.

crossReferences.note: The thirty pieces of silver and the potter's field are said to fulfill this prophecy, tied to Judas's betrayal price.
Judas agrees to betray Jesus for thirty pieces of silver, the Shepherd's insulting wage.
He came to His own, and His own did not receive Him, the rejection dramatized in this chapter.
The hireling who abandons the sheep contrasts with the good Shepherd, like the worthless shepherd here.

study.oneStory: The good Shepherd is rejected and valued at a slave's price, so grace and union are broken and a worthless shepherd takes His place. This is fulfilled when Christ is betrayed for thirty pieces of silver and spurned by His own. The story bends toward the cross, where the rejected Shepherd lays down His life for the sheep, and looks ahead to the judgment of every false shepherd who devours the flock.

quiz.q: What do the two staffs, Favor and Union, represent?

options.text: God's gracious protection and the God-given unity of His people.
options.note: Favor is gracious protection, Union the bond of brotherhood, both broken at rejection.

options.text: The wealth and armies of the nations.
options.note: They represent God's grace and the people's unity, not worldly power.

options.text: Two literal weapons for battle.
options.note: They are symbolic shepherd's staffs, not weapons.

options.text: Nothing in particular.
options.note: They clearly symbolize grace and union, both cut in two.

quiz.q: What price was weighed out as the shepherd's wages?

options.text: Thirty pieces of silver, the price of a slave, an insulting valuation.
options.note: Thirty pieces of silver was the compensation for a slave in Exodus.

options.text: A generous fortune in gold.
options.note: The sum was a contemptibly low insult, not a fortune.

options.text: Nothing at all.
options.note: They did weigh out wages, thirty pieces of silver.

options.text: A share of the temple treasury.
options.note: It was thirty pieces of silver, later thrown to the potter.

quiz.q: How is this prophecy fulfilled in the New Testament?

options.text: Judas betrayed Jesus for thirty pieces of silver, later used to buy the potter's field.
options.note: Matthew 27 cites this prophecy for the silver and the potter's field.

options.text: Jesus paid thirty pieces of silver to His disciples.
options.note: The silver was the betrayal price paid to Judas, not paid by Jesus.

options.text: The Romans returned thirty coins to Israel.
options.note: The fulfillment is Judas's betrayal price and the potter's field.

options.text: It is never fulfilled.
options.note: Matthew explicitly ties it to Christ's betrayal.

quiz.q: What happens when the good shepherd is rejected?

options.text: Grace and union are broken, and a worthless, devouring shepherd is raised up.
options.note: The staffs are cut in two and a foolish shepherd takes his place.

options.text: The flock immediately prospers on its own.
options.note: The opposite: they are handed over to a devouring shepherd.

options.text: God abandons the world entirely.
options.note: God judges through bad leadership but still pronounces woe on the false shepherd.

options.text: Nothing changes at all.
options.note: Grace and union are broken and a worthless shepherd arises.

quiz.q: What is pronounced on the worthless shepherd?

options.text: Woe, with judgment on his arm and right eye for deserting the flock.
options.note: Verse 17 pronounces woe and judgment on the worthless shepherd.

options.text: Blessing and long life.
options.note: The opposite: woe and judgment, not blessing.

options.text: Promotion to king.
options.note: He is judged with woe, not exalted.

options.text: Nothing is said about him.
options.note: A clear woe and judgment are pronounced on him.

questions: The people valued the good Shepherd at a slave's price. In what subtle ways might you undervalue Christ and what He has done for you?
When the Shepherd was rejected, grace and unity were broken. Where do you see the loss of God's grace or of unity where His care has been spurned?
Christ came to His own and was not received. How does the cost of His rejection deepen your gratitude for His faithfulness to you?
God gave the people over to a devouring shepherd. How careful are you about whose leadership and voices you allow to shape your soul?
The whole betrayal was foretold in exact detail. How does God's sovereignty over even Christ's betrayal strengthen your trust in His larger plan?

translationNotes.note: The two staffs are named Favor, noam, and Union, chovlim; some render Union as Bonds. They picture God's gracious protection and the unity of His people, both later cut in two.
Thirty pieces of silver was the compensation set for a slave gored by an ox in Exodus 21:32, a contemptibly low valuation of the Shepherd, cited in Matthew 27:9.
Throw it to the potter renders a gesture of contemptuous disposal at the temple; Matthew 27:7-10 records the betrayal money buying the potter's field, tying the two together.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 11:1-17. All 17 verses reproduced in order. One source em dash in verse 13 (Throw it to the potter, this magnificent price) rendered with a comma and closing quote per style.
